title: Robinson by Muriel Spark - Book Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CMh4ttABaiE

Daniel (@dungeondive) ventures outside his genre comfort zone to champion Muriel Spark—specifically her 1958 novel Robinson. Three survivors, one woman and two men, crash-land on a remote Atlantic island already inhabited by a man named Robinson and a mysterious boy named Miguel. The island itself is shaped like a chalk outline of a corpse, and our protagonist's surname is Marlo (yes, like Philip Marlowe). If you're expecting Robinson Crusoe survival fare, Spark has other plans.

This is allegory wearing the mask of a murder mystery, examining modernity versus conservatism, superstition versus spirituality, and colonialism—yet the characters never feel like mere vessels for ideas. Spark's command of suspense would make Hitchcock jealous, and Daniel is genuinely baffled that she never appeared in his women's literature courses. She crams more wit, dread, and theological unease into under 200 pages than most authors manage in trilogies. If you like fiction where the supernatural feels perpetually about to arrive but never quite does, Spark is your author.

----

What's your favourite work of literary suspense that genre readers might overlook?
